shortDescription.text: HMAC signature compared with non-constant-time string equality

fullDescription.text: The HMAC-SHA256 signature verification uses Python's '!=' operator for string comparison instead of hmac.compare_digest(). This exposes the comparison to timing side-channel attacks, where an attacker can measure response times to incrementally guess each byte of the valid signature. Although exploitation requires many precise measurements, this is a well-known cryptographic weakness with a straightforward fix.

shortDescription.text: Nonce not tracked server-side, allowing signed URL replay within timeout window

fullDescription.text: The file download signature verification accepts a nonce parameter but never stores or checks it against previously used values. A valid signed URL can be replayed unlimited times within the FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT window. While the timeout limits the attack window, server-side nonce tracking is required to prevent replay attacks entirely. An intercepted or leaked signed URL grants repeated file access until expiration.

shortDescription.text: HMAC signing falls back to empty key when SECRET_KEY is not configured

fullDescription.text: When dify_config.SECRET_KEY is None or empty, the HMAC signing and verification uses an empty byte string (b'') as the secret key. This means any attacker can forge valid signatures for arbitrary file IDs by computing HMAC-SHA256 with an empty key. This effectively disables all signature-based access control on file uploads and downloads.

shortDescription.text: Upload endpoint trusts client-supplied MIME type without server-side content validation

fullDescription.text: The plugin file upload endpoint uses file.mimetype (provided by the HTTP client) directly as the stored MIME type without performing any server-side content-type detection (e.g., magic bytes / libmagic). While the HMAC signature binds the mimetype, the original signed URL is also generated from a plugin-supplied mimetype value. A compromised or malicious plugin can request a signed URL for 'image/png' but upload an HTML or SVG file with that MIME type claim. The file is then stored and served with the spoofed MIME type, potentially enabling content-type confusion attacks downstream.

shortDescription.text: enforce_download_for_html does not block SVG files which can execute JavaScript

fullDescription.text: The enforce_download_for_html function only blocks HTML and XHTML MIME types (text/html, application/xhtml+xml) and extensions (.html, .htm). SVG files (image/svg+xml, .svg) can contain embedded JavaScript via <script> tags or event handlers (onload, onclick). If an attacker uploads an SVG file (potentially via MIME spoofing), it will be served inline with its original MIME type, enabling stored XSS. Other dangerous types like application/xml with XSLT are also not covered.

shortDescription.text: File content is fully read into memory before size limits are enforced

fullDescription.text: In the upload handler, file.read() loads the entire file into memory and passes it to ToolFileManager.create_file_by_raw(). The FileTooLargeError is only raised after the file has already been fully read and potentially stored. This allows an attacker to cause memory exhaustion by uploading very large files, even if the size limit eventually rejects the file. The size check should occur before reading the file body, ideally via Content-Length header validation or streaming with an early cutoff.

shortDescription.text: Signature timestamp validation does not reject future timestamps

fullDescription.text: The verify_tool_file_signature and verify_plugin_file_signature functions check that current_time - int(timestamp) <= FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT, but do not check for negative values (i.e., timestamps in the future). An attacker who obtains or forges a signature with a far-future timestamp can create signed URLs that remain valid indefinitely, bypassing the intended expiration mechanism. The check should be abs(current_time - int(timestamp)) <= FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT or explicitly reject future timestamps.

message.text: HMAC signature verification uses '!=' string comparison instead of hmac.compare_digest(). This enables timing side-channel attacks to incrementally recover valid signatures. Replace 'if sign != recalculated_encoded_sign' with 'if not hmac.compare_digest(sign, recalculated_encoded_sign)'.

HMAC signature verification uses '!=' string comparison instead of hmac.compare_digest(). This enables timing side-channel attacks. Replace 'if sign != recalculated_encoded_sign' with 'if not hmac.compare_digest(sign, recalculated_encoded_sign)'.

HMAC signature verification in ToolFileManager.verify_file uses '!=' string comparison instead of hmac.compare_digest(). This is a duplicate implementation of the same vulnerable pattern found in signature.py.

verify_tool_file_signature accepts a nonce parameter but never records it server-side. The same (timestamp, nonce, sign) tuple can be reused unlimited times within the FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT window. Implement server-side nonce tracking (e.g., Redis set with TTL matching the timeout) to prevent replay.

When dify_config.SECRET_KEY is None or empty, HMAC falls back to b'' as the signing key: `secret_key = dify_config.SECRET_KEY.encode() if dify_config.SECRET_KEY else b""`. An attacker can trivially forge all signatures. Add a startup check that SECRET_KEY is set and has sufficient entropy, or raise an error if it is empty.
Duplicate empty-key fallback in ToolFileManager.sign_file and verify_file. Same vulnerability as in signature.py — empty SECRET_KEY means all signed URLs are forgeable.

The upload endpoint uses file.mimetype directly from the HTTP request without server-side content-type detection (magic bytes). The HMAC signature binds the mimetype, but the original value originates from the plugin which may be compromised. A malicious plugin can claim any MIME type for arbitrary file content, bypassing downstream content-type-based security controls.

enforce_download_for_html only blocks text/html and application/xhtml+xml MIME types. SVG files (image/svg+xml) can contain embedded <script> tags and event handlers for JavaScript execution. If an SVG is uploaded and served inline, this enables stored XSS. Add image/svg+xml to HTML_MIME_TYPES and .svg to HTML_EXTENSIONS, or implement a Content-Security-Policy header.

The tool_files.py download endpoint relies on enforce_download_for_html to prevent XSS, but SVG files are not covered. An uploaded SVG with embedded JavaScript will be served inline with Content-Type: image/svg+xml, executing scripts in the user's browser context.

file.read() at the upload endpoint loads the entire file body into memory before ToolFileManager.create_file_by_raw stores it and before any size validation occurs. An attacker can exhaust server memory by sending a large request body. Validate Content-Length header or use streaming with an early cutoff before fully reading the file.

verify_tool_file_signature checks 'current_time - int(timestamp) <= FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT' but does not reject future timestamps. A forged signature with timestamp far in the future (e.g., year 2099) will produce a large negative difference, which is always <= timeout. This creates effectively non-expiring signed URLs. Add a check: 'if int(timestamp) > current_time: return False'.
verify_plugin_file_signature has the same future-timestamp bypass. A plugin-forged signature with a future timestamp will never expire.
